1.description: The value of commitment and intertemporal inefficiency. This plot shows how the value of commitment (ֿ•גˆ—גˆ—$\phi ^{**}$) in Panel A and the degree of intertemporal inefficiency (X1גˆ—X1גˆ—גˆ—$\frac{X_{1}^{*}}{X_{1}^{**}}$) in Panel B varies with the degree of self-interest within the household (־”$\Delta$). The plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=50$T=50$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the intertemporal elasticity of substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$. Since the utility function is homothetic, both outcomes are invariant to the level ofW1$W_{1}$. Both figuresֲ are drawn for the case where household members place low, intermediate, and high weight on the value of public consumption (־¼H=0,0.4,0.8$\mu _{H}=0,0.4,0.8$). These comparative statics are generated assuming household wealth is shared between both members. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Time-inconsistency with separate accounts. This plot shows the degree of overconsumption (Panel A) and value of commitment (Panel B) att=1$t=1$when household members save in separate accounts. Without loss of generality, both plots show outcomes over the range of relative wealth whereW1,AW1גˆˆ12,1$\frac{W_{1,A}}{W_{1}}\in \left[\frac{1}{2},1\right]$since the equilibrium outcomes below this range are symmetric with this case. For comparison the value of commitment under joint accounts is also shown (invariant to the distribution of relative wealth between the members). Each plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=3$T=3$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the intertemporal elasticity of substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, the degree of risk aversion is unity־³=1$\gamma =1$, the elasticity between each member's private utility is unity־¾=1$\xi =1$, the standard deviation of the relative wealth shock isֿƒ=0.1$\sigma =0.1$, the degree of self-interest within the household is־”=0.4$\varDelta =0.4$, the utility weight on the public good is־¼H=0.4$\mu _{H}=0.4$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Time-inconsistency with separate accountsג€”Decomposed. These plots decompose the time-inconsistent consumption choices of the household with separate accounts att=1$t=1$. Panel A shows the total level of private consumption relative to the full-commitment Pareto optimum. Panel B shows the level of individual private consumption of each member measured relative to total household wealth att=1$t=1$. Panel C shows the total level of public consumption relative to the full-commitment Pareto optimum. Without loss of generality, both plots show outcomes over the range of relative wealth whereW1,AW1גˆˆ12,1$\frac{W_{1,A}}{W_{1}}\in \left[\frac{1}{2},1\right]$since the equilibrium outcomes below this range are symmetric with this case. For comparison, each plot also contains the equilibrium outcome with joint accounts (invariant to the relative wealth of each member). Each plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=3$T=3$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the intertemporal elasticity of substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, the degree of risk aversion is unity־³=1$\gamma =1$, the elasticity between each member's private utility is unity־¾=1$\xi =1$, the standard deviation of the relative wealth shock isֿƒ=0.1$\sigma =0.1$, the degree of self-interest within the household is־”=0.4$\varDelta =0.4$, the utility weight on the public good is־¼H=0.4$\mu _{H}=0.4$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Marginal propensity to consume with separate accounts. This plot shows the marginal propensity to consume for total household expenditure (Panel A) and expenditure on the public good (Panel B) att=1$t=1$. The marginal propensity is shown based on whether an incremental dollar is given to memberA$A$, memberB$B$, or both household members in proportion to their initial wealth. Without loss of generality, this plot is shown over the range of relative wealth whereW1,AW1גˆˆ12,1$\frac{W_{1,A}}{W_{1}}\in \left[\frac{1}{2},1\right]$since the equilibrium outcomes below this range are symmetric with this case. Each plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=3$T=3$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the intertemporal elasticity of substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, the degree of risk aversion is unity־³=1$\gamma =1$, the elasticity between each member's private utility is unity־¾=1$\xi =1$, the standard deviation of the relative wealth shock isֿƒ=0.1$\sigma =0.1$, the degree of self-interest within the household is־”=0.4$\varDelta =0.4$, the utility weight on the public good is־¼H=0.4$\mu _{H}=0.4$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Equilibrium with endogenous account selection. This plot shows equilibrium outcomes att=1$t=1$when household members can endogenously choose each period whether to save using a joint or separate account. Without loss of generality, this plot is shown over the range of relative wealth whereW1,AW1גˆˆ12,1$\frac{W_{1,A}}{W_{1}}\in \left[\frac{1}{2},1\right]$since the equilibrium outcomes below this range are symmetric with this case. Each plot indicates the threshold at which the household elects to adopt a joint account as well as the threshold at which the member with more wealth transfers wealth to the other att=1$t=1$. Panel A shows the equilibrium private consumption of each member, Panel B shows total household expenditure measured relative to the first-best full commitment solution, and Panel C shows the value of commitment. Each plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=3$T=3$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the elasticity of intertemporal substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, the degree of risk aversion is unity־³=1$\gamma =1$, the elasticity between each member's private utility is unity־¾=1$\xi =1$, the degree of self-interest within the household is־”=0.4$\Delta =0.4$, the utility weight on the public good is־¼H=0.4$\mu _{H}=0.4$, the standard deviation of the relative wealth shock isֿƒ=0.15$\sigma =0.15$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Account choice and intra-household risk. This plot shows the threshold of the relative wealth distribution below which the household will elect to save in a joint account att=1$t=1$. The threshold is shown for different levels of the standard deviation of the relative wealth shockֿƒ$\sigma$. Without loss of generality, the threshold shown in terms of the relative wealth of memberA$A$W1,AW1גˆˆ12,1$\frac{W_{1,A}}{W_{1}}\in \left[\frac{1}{2},1\right]$and a symmetric threshold exists forB$B$below this range. Each plot is drawn using the following parameters: Household members have exponential discount factor of־´=0.95$\delta =0.95$, the gross interest rate isR=1/0.95$R=1/0.95$, the household exists forT=3$T=3$years, the elasticity of substitution between goods is unity־µ=1$\varepsilon =1$, the intertemporal elasticity of substitution is unityz=1$z=1$, the degree of risk aversion is unity־³=1$\gamma =1$, the elasticity between each member's private utility is unity־¾=1$\xi =1$, the degree of self-interest within the household is־”=0.4$\varDelta =0.4$, the utility weight on the public good is־¼H=0.4$\mu _{H}=0.4$, and the relative price of the public good is onepH=1$p_{H}=1$.
